author: lightclient
content:
Not sure if i fully grok this. The sig list as currently proposed allows a sig to either be 1) over the canonical sig hash of the tx or 2) over a specified message value. 

The entries in the signature list include signature metadata like who the expected signer is. So it isn't really possible to move the signature around. The canonical signature hash is defined as the serialized value of the tx, just with the actual type 1) sig bytes elided. So the address to recover is locked into a certain position and the type 1) sigs are over the type 2) sig data

author: _shemnon
content:
This is because the frame tx does something important: it extracts no data from the sig other than the validity over the data. All prior tx formats extract the execution address and use that as data, for frames the relevant address is part of the tx body (which is essential for account abstraction, and separating the signatre algorithm from the address). i.e. each frame has an address and the EVM execution is from that TX provided address, whereas all others to date extract the address from pubkey extraction from the signature.  Change the signature you change the address in 0x02 TXes, not so with 8141.

So as long as this holds (tx sig only provides valid/invaid signal) the signature substitution attacks are mitigated. Signing over different values is defense in depth in this case, but still leaves open some validation griefing that can be done. i.e. if the mempool validates the sig against the hash w/o the because it recognizes the frame is a Falcon signature, and pubkey and sig are in the sig data already, it may pass the mempool but the EVM execution may reject it because the pubkey hash doesn't match or some other data dependant reason.  In this case frame sigs can be swapped or duplicated, get past the mempool, and fail in EVM evaluation. Making each frame position sign a different hash ends this griefing.

author: _shemnon
content: I've also flipped on addresses, I used to think they needed to be a hash of the pubkey, but full account abstraction needs to sever that tie too. It's a good place to start and is useful for counterfactual addresses, but there are other counterfactual methods that can ensure only a specific key can be used at an address, mostly involving the hash fed into the CREATE2 when the account is created is created. This is another advantage frame tx has over other proposals.
